authoritative_sources.title: Campus LAN and Wireless LAN Solution Design Guide
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cisco Design Zone publishes validated enterprise campus design guidance from a major enterprise networking platform vendor, making it authoritative for campus topology, access/distribution/core patterns, resiliency, segmentation, and operational deployment terminology.
used_for: evidence expectations for access, distribution, core, wired, and wireless design

authoritative_sources.title: Cisco Validated: Design, Deploy, and Operate
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cisco's official validated design portal is a public operating reference for enterprise networking architecture patterns across campus, SD-WAN, data center, and access networking.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: reference architecture selection and materialization caveat review
used_for: vendor-validated enterprise network architecture pattern selection
refresh anchor for vendor-specific design references

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 4271 - A Border Gateway Protocol 4 (BGP-4)
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 4271 is the standards-track BGP-4 specification for inter-AS route exchange, path attributes, RIB behavior, route advertisement, and policy-constrained route selection.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: BGP edge failover and route-policy review

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 2328 - OSPF Version 2
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 2328 is the standards-track OSPFv2 specification governing areas, adjacencies, LSAs, route calculation, and convergence behavior.

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 1034 - Domain Names: Concepts and Facilities
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 1034 defines DNS architecture concepts, zones, delegation, resolvers, and cache behavior used to reason about enterprise DNS design and failover effects.

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 2131 - Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 2131 is the standards-track DHCP specification for client-server state, leases, address allocation, and configuration parameter delivery.
used_for: lease, scope, and relay evidence requirements
safe refusal when DHCP authority records are missing

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 5880 - Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD)
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 5880 defines BFD as a protocol-independent failure-detection mechanism relevant to high-availability routing design and fast path-failure detection.

authoritative_sources.title: Security Policy Best Practices
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Palo Alto Networks is a firewall platform publisher; its official guide is authoritative for firewall policy planning, deployment, rule hygiene, logging, inspection, and maintenance on its platform.

authoritative_sources.title: Route table concepts - Amazon Virtual Private Cloud
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS is the platform publisher; its VPC route-table documentation defines subnet associations, destinations, targets, main route tables, custom route tables, and local routing behavior that govern cloud network path decisions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud edge and hybrid routing review

authoritative_sources.title: Creating Amazon Route 53 health checks and configuring DNS failover
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS is the Route 53 platform publisher; its documentation defines health-check and DNS failover behavior for Route 53 routing-policy decisions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: DNS failover and health-check tradeoff review

authoritative_sources.title: Flow log records - Amazon Virtual Private Cloud
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS is the platform publisher; its flow-log record documentation defines cloud network telemetry fields and limitations used as evidence for reachability and policy troubleshooting.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: connectivity troubleshooting and monitoring evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-41 Rev. 1 - Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is a public standards body; SP 800-41 Rev. 1 is recognized guidance for firewall policy, firewall management, rule review, logging, and perimeter-control evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: firewall control evidence and policy-review caveats

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-207 - Zero Trust Architecture
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-207 is recognized public guidance for zero trust concepts that influence enterprise network segmentation, policy-enforcement points, and trust-boundary design.

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is a recognized cross-industry governance, protection, detection, response, and recovery framework that supports network architecture risk and evidence framing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: risk, monitoring, response, and recovery evidence mapping

workflow_stages.name: Intake and evidence prerequisite check
workflow_stages.description: Confirm network scope, tenant authority, topology, IPAM, DNS, DHCP, route, firewall, load-balancer, monitoring, incident, change, criticality, and rollback evidence before offering definitive guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Escalate when required facts or systems of record are missing, stale, inaccessible, or contradictory.

workflow_stages.name: Topology and trust-domain review
workflow_stages.description: Review zones, VLANs, subnets, WAN, cloud edge, management paths, perimeter controls, blast-radius boundaries, and policy-enforcement points against enterprise design and zero-trust evidence.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Route to identity, endpoint, security architecture, or human review when trust decisions depend on controls outside network architecture.

workflow_stages.name: Routing, DNS, DHCP, and edge analysis
workflow_stages.description: Assess BGP, OSPF, BFD, route tables, DNS zones, resolver paths, DHCP scopes and relays, firewall policy, NAT, load-balancing, and WAN path evidence.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Stop when protocol or platform evidence is insufficient to distinguish observed behavior from assumption.

workflow_stages.name: Corrective guidance and troubleshooting handoff
workflow_stages.description: Produce operator-safe remediation guidance, validation criteria, monitoring gaps, runbooks, confidence statements, rollback checkpoints, and owner handoffs without making live changes.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Refuse live execution, credential handling, exploit validation, forensic collection, or final approval.

workflow_stages.name: Boundary escalation or refusal
workflow_stages.description: Return to orchestrator when work shifts to endpoint, identity, application, incident-response, forensics, legal, compliance, procurement, or production execution authority.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Refuse out-of-scope work clearly and provide a handoff packet when useful.

artifacts.traceability_expectation: Map every recommendation to trust boundaries, source IDs, tenant records, assumptions, and validation gates.

evidence_required: route, DNS, DHCP, firewall, WAN, or load-balancer evidence
artifacts.traceability_expectation: Separate immediate containment, durable design correction, operational risk, and required human execution authority.

evidence_required: routing, DNS, DHCP, firewall, load-balancer, and WAN records
artifacts.traceability_expectation: List hypotheses, confidence level, validation steps, missing evidence, and handoff owner.

evidence_required: flow logs, syslog, SNMP, synthetic checks, health checks, and packet evidence
artifacts.traceability_expectation: Map telemetry gaps to routing, DNS, DHCP, firewall, WAN, cloud edge, load-balancing, deployment readiness, and rollback needs.
Name the refusal trigger, retained network-scope question if any, handoff owner, and no-action boundary.

systems_of_record.governs: topology, trust domains, VLANs, subnets, WAN, edge, and ownership context

systems_of_record.system: IPAM, DNS, and DHCP management systems
systems_of_record.governs: address allocation, zone ownership, resolver path, DHCP scopes, relay paths, leases, and naming assumptions

systems_of_record.system: router, switch, firewall, load-balancer, SD-WAN, and cloud-network configuration repositories or approved exports
systems_of_record.governs: route tables, route policy, firewall policy, NAT, load-balancing, WAN path, cloud route-table, and perimeter behavior

systems_of_record.system: monitoring, logging, flow-log, syslog, SNMP, synthetic-check, packet-capture, and health-check platforms
systems_of_record.governs: operational evidence, reachability symptoms, policy effects, detection coverage, and deployment readiness

systems_of_record.system: change-management, incident-management, problem-management, and architecture decision record repositories
systems_of_record.governs: change windows, rollback authority, incident timeline, recurrence, problem ownership, and final approval boundary

systems_of_record.system: tenant policy, risk, compliance, contractual availability, and approval records
systems_of_record.governs: availability commitments, security baselines, exception handling, human signoff, legal boundaries, and tenant adaptation rules

allowed: Prepare evidence-backed enterprise network architecture recommendations.
Recommend routing, segmentation, DNS, DHCP, firewall, WAN, edge, and load-balancing design changes for authorized operators to review.
Rank network architecture risks and specify validation, rollback, and monitoring evidence.
Prepare troubleshooting runbooks and incident-containment handoff guidance.
Map network architecture artifacts to readiness and evidence gaps without certifying compliance.
escalate: Required topology, route, DNS, DHCP, firewall, load-balancer, telemetry, criticality, or authority records are missing.
Public guidance, tenant policy, contractual requirements, or change authority conflict.
Confidence is low because assumptions dominate the available evidence.
Final approval, exception acceptance, compliance signoff, or emergency change authority is requested.
The lane needs a more specialized network, security, cloud, identity, endpoint, application, or incident-response owner.
refuse: Endpoint, identity, or device-management security control design or execution.
Application-only performance tuning unrelated to network path behavior.
Penetration-testing execution, exploit validation, or forensic workflow.
Live network device, firewall, load-balancer, cloud route-table, DNS, or DHCP change execution.
Legal or compliance policy language outside network architecture evidence mapping.
Credential handling or secrets intake for production devices.

domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Enterprise networks often fail through trust-boundary ambiguity, lateral movement paths, management-plane exposure, and broad allow rules.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: routing recommendation based on incomplete protocol evidence
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: BGP, OSPF, BFD, route-table, and policy interactions can create outages when path selection, convergence, or summarization behavior is assumed rather than evidenced.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: DNS or DHCP guidance that ignores authority, cache, lease, scope, or relay state
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Name resolution and address assignment failures can appear as application or endpoint issues while being governed by network records and protocol state.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: telemetry treated as complete when it is delayed, aggregated, absent, or owned by another system
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Flow logs, health checks, syslog, SNMP, packet captures, and synthetic checks differ in precision and delay; weak observability can make confident remediation unsafe.
Network architecture guidance becomes unsafe when it turns into endpoint control, identity policy, application optimization, security testing, forensics, legal signoff, or live change execution.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: tenant adaptation rewrites the public baseline without evidence
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Cross-industry enterprise networking must adapt to tenant systems and policies, but unsupported local terminology can hide missing source records or policy conflicts.

required_workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Network source-of-truth, IPAM, DNS/DHCP, route-policy, firewall, load-balancer, SD-WAN, cloud-network, monitoring, incident, change, ADR, policy, risk, compliance, contractual availability, and approval systems govern safe recommendations.

required_workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Enterprise network architecture fails through protocol state, trust-boundary ambiguity, routing convergence, address and name authority, telemetry limitations, stateful inspection, WAN asymmetry, and production change risk.

required_workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Endpoint, identity, device-management, application-only performance, security testing, incident response, forensics, legal, compliance signoff, procurement, finance, and live operations authority are outside the lane.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: vendor-specific command syntax and implementation behavior
unresolved_ambiguities.description: Exact CLI, API, or configuration syntax depends on vendor, platform version, licensing, and tenant change-control process.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Resolved at runtime by tenant-supplied platform exports and authorized operator review; this spec pack remains design and guidance only.

unresolved_ambiguities.description: Different tenants may have regulatory, contractual, insurance, or safety commitments that alter acceptable network risk and change windows.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Require tenant policy and authority records before final recommendations; escalate conflicts to compliance, legal, or risk owners.

unresolved_ambiguities.description: Platform route-table, flow-log, DNS failover, and load-balancing features can change on vendor cadence.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Per-source refresh intervals and decay policies require review before material recommendations that rely on provider behavior.

market_ready_research_conclusion.conclusion: The source set covers enterprise networking workflow, protocol behavior, platform evidence, firewall policy, segmentation, monitoring, risk framing, and refusal boundaries sufficiently for a validated specification-based package.
